    16. WRITING CAPTIONS (THE DEFINITIVE 10-STEP GUIDE - Even A Baby Can Do It)
</h2>

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=28981643, kind=text -->
  <div class="lecture-attachment lecture-attachment-type-text" id="lecture-attachment-28981643">
    <div class="attachment-data"></div>
    
      <div class="attachment-data"></div>
<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><blockquote>
<span style="color: rgb(38, 38, 38);">"It’s not that you’re “bad at captions”. You’re just bad at realizing how unique your voice is. And that you don’t have to be clever if you’re bringing us the truth. You’re bad at knowing that even if you don’t say things perfectly, your words may be someone else’s salvation. But one day, when you finally realize that your “brand” isn’t the people you’re trying to copy... but that it’s actually the person you’ve been hiding inside... Then you won’t mind being “bad at captions” because you’ll finally be excellent at something more important… Being yourself." </span><strong style="color: rgb(38, 38, 38);">Bronques</strong>
</blockquote><p><br></p><p>So many people struggle with writing captions.</p><p>They want to post something but they have no idea what to write so they settle for emoji's... </p><p>Or they steal someone else's quote...</p><p>Or they copy the "basic bitch" phrases you see all over Instagram. </p><p>You too, may have been fooled into thinking that you have to be a writer, or super witty to do write great captions.</p><p>No, all you need is a process. </p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP ONE: PREPARATION</strong></h1><p>(DAYS BEFORE)</p><p>Collect ideas.</p><p>When rappers or musicians go into writing sessions, they bring a notebook of ideas. Or a beat, or a riff so they always have a place to start.</p><p>You should do the same. </p><p>Write down ideas you get as you go through your daily life. . Put them in your NOTES app on your phone These will be great jump-off points for your captions later. </p><p><br></p><p>(HOURS BEFORE)</p><p>Get off social media.</p><p>Read writing that inspires you.</p><p>Read passages from books you love (prose not poetry -- poetry isn't as conversational as we'll need you to be here).</p><p>Get in the zone of what good writing actually reads like.</p><p><br></p><p>(JUST BEFORE)</p><p>Close your laptop. Turn off all devices.</p><p>Print out those ideas you've been collecting.</p><p>Get a pen and paper.</p><p>You might wanna fight me one this, but try it. Writing with "pen and paper" makes ideas flow faster than using a laptop or the phone. It's counter-intuitive, but this "old school" way is the fastest way. Especially if you don't "really know what to write".</p><p>It's like the pen becomes a magic wand for ideas. I don't know why, but it works. And if speed is key for you like it is for me, there is no other way. And the side benefit is your writing gets this soulfulness that other people's lacks. </p><p>My friend India, who makes a living writing captions, writes by hand too. Check out this video. Even when she was building her website... She used pen and paper: </p><p></p>
</div>

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=28981781, kind=text -->
  <div class="lecture-attachment lecture-attachment-type-text" id="lecture-attachment-28981781">
    <div class="attachment-data"></div>
    
      <div class="attachment-data"></div>
<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p>It's just a snippet. But I had to share because I think this step is important.</p><p>Ok. Now, get cozy.</p><p>Treat caption writing with the same respect that you'd give to any other creative activity.</p><p><em>"Caption writing is an art form, bro."</em></p><p>Don't believe me? Here's a <a href="https://www.nytimes.com/2019/03/27/style/instagram-long-captions.html" target="_blank">New York Times article</a> for some inspiration.</p><p>The words you put down today could become a script or a book one day. You never know. Get in the "caption writing zone". 'Cause trust me, when it starts flowing there's no stopping it.</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP TWO: WRITE DOWN EVERYTHING YOU SEE</strong></h1><p>Make a list, on the left side of the page, of everything you see or notice in the picture. Objects, expressions, oddities, etc...</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP THREE: WRITE DOWN EVERYTHING YOU DON'T SEE</strong></h1><p>Make a list, on the right hand side of everything you DON'T see in the picture. Like, where you are... Or the people you know where there but aren't pictured... Or something interesting that you know (or imagine) that's happening out of frame.</p><p>Is there an important lesson you can teach here?</p><p>Is there something about the moment in this photo that inspires you?</p><p>Something you remember that still puts a smaile on your face?</p><p>Behind the scenes information? </p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP FOUR: PUT BOXES AROUND THE IDEAS YOU WROTE THAT STICK OUT TO YOU</strong></h1><p>Basquiat does this a lot in his paintings. He puts boxes around key words. It has a magical affect on your psychology.</p><p>Do it with your list of key words</p><p>Look at your list and put a box around the things that have the most emotional resonance.</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP FIVE: DECIDE WHO YOU'RE WRITING TO</strong></h1><p>Writing to a particular person is the ultimate framing device.</p><p>If you write captions directed to the masses, they'll always be so vanilla. Instead, choose a person to direct your writing to. A real person. Preferably a friend.</p><p>And then write at the top of a brand new page...</p><p>Dear (your friends name),</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP SIX: WRITE ABOUT THE THINGS YOU'VE HIGHLIGHTED</strong></h1><p>Write to your friend about each of the things you've highlighted.</p><p>These ideas don't have to relate to each other yet. Write about whatever reason these things have an emotional connection with you. Conversational.</p><p>Tell stories about these things. Give your reader interesting facts about them. Share why these things resonate with you. Or why you think they would resonate with the person you're writing to.</p><p>And remember. Write casual. Like you're writing a text.</p><p>The way our teachers taught us to write in English class is the wrong way to write captions. This is not an essay. It's you talking. To a friend.</p><p>Make it transcription of your thoughts.</p><p>Make it conversational. You don't need fancy words.</p><p>Imagine a guy who's wife just found out she's pregnant. Does it matter how you tell him? You could use broken english to say it or the fanciest words... the effect will be the same: he's gonna be glad you told him.</p><p>The content of your words is more important than how you say it.</p><p>Don't second guess this stuff.</p><p>There is no 'one' caption. Let the Gods guide you one what energy this caption needs to have TODAY.</p><p>Every single person who would write about the things you highlighted... Would write something different. Tomorrow YOU would write something different.</p><p>What makes a caption good or not is how much of YOU that you put into it. The more of "your truth" that you inject, the more captivating it will be to a reader.</p><p>Don’t think that no one will care about this super specific thing you’re writing about. If you’re passionate about the subject you're writing about, someone else out there will care too. That's the beauty of being so connected on the internet.</p><p>But you know that already.</p><p>Nah, the real thing you're worried about is the clump of people who will judge you. And that’s what freezes you up. But why are you playing to the people in the back when there are people in the front row standing on their seats who paid to see the whites of your eyes? </p><p>Connect with YOUR FANS first. Ignore the haters.</p><p>Your pure energy will eventually carry through all the way back and influence the world. </p><p><br></p><p><strong>NIJA TRICK: RECORD YOURSELF FREESTYLING</strong></p><p>If you’re one of those people who considers yourself a talker, but freeze up when you have to write a caption, then do this:</p><p>Record yourself "pretend talking" to your friend about what you've highlighted. Then transcribe the best bits of what you said.</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP SEVEN: LOOK AT YOUR RIFFS NOTEBOOK</strong></h1><p>Remember those ideas that you've been collecting?</p><p>Now's a good time to take a glance at them.</p><p>It's crazy how the most unrelated ideas, can become connected in a magical moment.</p><p>Or maybe one of those ideas sends your brain down a rabbit hole. You end up somewhere totally unrelated, but totally awesome. All good. Keep the ideas flowing.</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP EIGHT: KILL YOUR DARLINGS</strong></h1><p>There will be many ideas on your paper now. It's time to choose which one's will make it to the caption. If you've done a good job so far, you may find it hard to choose only one...</p><p>Kill your darlings: pick one idea and go with it.</p><p>All the other "sick caption ideas"? Add them to your "sick caption ideas notebook". When you use this method of writing captions, you always end up with more extra captions that you may be able to use later. So if you wrote a good metaphor that doesn't work here... don't worry, you can use it later for something else</p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP NINE: TAKE OUT THE 'DEAR XXX,' PART</strong></h1><p>When you're finished, cross out the "Dear XXX," part at the top.</p><p>You will now have a caption with real personality because you directed it to a real person.</p><p>People will feel like you wrote for them.</p><p><br></p><p class="ql-align-center"><img data-imageloader="" data-imageloader-src="https://uploads.teachablecdn.com/attachments/S9gLUWktRPiYWzIeqQIh_captionsSmall.png" style="max-width: 273px;" src="https://uploads.teachablecdn.com/attachments/S9gLUWktRPiYWzIeqQIh_captionsSmall.png" data-imageloader-current-size="" class="is-loaded"></p><p class="ql-align-center"><em>This is what it looks like when I'm working on 3 captions at a time.</em></p><p><br></p><h1><strong>STEP TEN: SIT ON THE CAPTION</strong></h1><p>If you have time... Don't put it up right away. Giving yourself a break to look at it again later can help you turn an already good caption into a real gem.</p><p><strong>NINJA TRICK</strong></p><p>Use the “magazine article approach”. Write caption first then find (or create) a picture that suits it.</p><p>If speed is your goal, this will be the fastest content creation process.</p><p>IF you love writing.</p><p>IF you have access to a photographer or you're good with getting photos for yourself.</p><p>Alright now... Go write some captions!</p><p>P.S. Don't expect crazy reactions right away. They may not even see your captions because you haven't "trained" them to look down there yet. Give it time. They'll think the first long caption is a fluke. People need to trust before they invest in your story. Soon, they will come to expect it.</p><p><br></p><p></p>
</div>
